data.title: Ridge Security Brings Validated Attack Insights into CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM
data.description:
Ridge Security Brings Validated Attack Insights into CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM

Ridge Security today announced an integration that enables automated penetration testing insights from RidgeBot® to flow into CrowdStrike Falcon® Next-Gen SIEM.

As organizations face increasing volumes of vulnerabilities and alerts, security teams often lack clarity into which exposures represent real, exploitable risk. By bringing RidgeBot’s validated attack findings into the CrowdStrike Falcon® platform, organizations can prioritize remediation based on proven attack paths and reduce noise across security operations.

The integration enables automated penetration testing results from RidgeBot to be ingested into Falcon Next-Gen SIEM, where they can be correlated with other security data to provide a centralized view of validated cyber risk.

“Security teams need a clear view of which vulnerabilities represent real risk,” said Lydia Zhang, co-founder and president of Ridge Security. “By integrating RidgeBot with CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM, we’re helping organizations bring validated attack insights directly into their workflows so they can prioritize and remediate threats faster.”

Availability

The RidgeBot Data Connector for Falcon Next-Gen SIEM is available today through the CrowdStrike Marketplace. To learn more, watch the integration overview video or download the solution brief, “Accelerating Risk Detection with RidgeBot and CrowdStrike Falcon NGSIEM.”

About Ridge Security

Ridge Security delivers intelligent, autonomous cybersecurity validation solutions that help organizations proactively manage risk and improve resilience. Its flagship platform, RidgeBot®, is a leading agentic AI-based adversarial risk validation platform that supports continuous threat exposure management programs. Recognized by Gartner in the Market Guide for Adversarial Exposure Validation and honored with awards including Top Emerging Cyber Security Company, CRN’s Tech Elite 250, and the TMCnet Cybersecurity Excellence award, Ridge Security serves customers worldwide across finance, government, telecom, and enterprise sectors.

For more information, go to ridgesecurity.ai.

Media Contacts

Ridge Security

data.title: Helena Capital Welcomes Katy Nelson as General Partner to Advance Its Next Phase of Growth
data.description:
Helena Capital Welcomes Katy Nelson as General Partner to Advance Its Next Phase of Growth

Former Andreessen Horowitz Partner joins Sarah Antor and Robin Wood Sailer to deepen the firm’s transatlantic operating partnership across critical technology and resilience sectors

Helena Capital today announced that Katy Nelson has joined Sarah Antor and Robin Wood Sailer as General Partner. Nelson’s addition marks a significant milestone for the firm, bringing an unparalleled depth of experience and strategic insight that will be instrumental in shaping its future and rounding out the management team.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260429241395/en/

Katy Nelson, General Partner, Helena Capital

Nelson joins Helena Capital after a distinguished five-year tenure at Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), where she served as a Partner. During her time there, she was the architect and leader of the firm’s largest cross-sector go-to-market (GTM) organization. This initiative was pivotal in helping a16z scale its assets under management from approximately $13 billion to over $90 billion. Nelson was a vital part of establishing GTM as a core operating function within a16z, providing essential support to hundreds of portfolio companies – from nascent, pre-product startups through to those preparing for IPO. Her work in this capacity provided her with a 360-degree view of enterprise growth dynamics.

Within the a16z ecosystem, Nelson worked extensively with the firm’s cybersecurity portfolio, where she built a robust global CISO (Chief Information Security Officer) network and community. This direct engagement has given her profound, first-hand insight into how security and identity leaders evaluate, adopt, and deploy cutting-edge enterprise technology.

Nelson brings a perspective that is highly relevant to Helena Capital and its portfolio companies. At Andreessen Horowitz, she played a key role in the early development and scaling of the firm’s groundbreaking efforts to invest in companies critical to the U.S. national interest. She helped shape how the firm engaged with companies operating in sectors central to national resilience and industrial capacity, and was instrumental in supporting the deployment of capital into this vision. Her work there focused on translating technical innovation into real-world adoption through enterprise go-to-market, partnerships, and institutional engagement.

“Katy Nelson is one of the most respected operators in venture capital, and her arrival represents a significant inflection point for Helena Capital,” said Sarah Antor, Managing Partner of Helena Capital. “Robin and I built this firm around a clear conviction: the next generation of category-defining companies in advanced manufacturing, supply chain, and resilient infrastructure will be won not just on capital, but on hands-on operational partnership. Katy embodies that thesis. She has the rare combination of investor judgment, operator instinct, and global network that our portfolio companies need to scale across the Atlantic.”

At Helena Capital, the mission is to build a distinctive transatlantic fund that actively bridges the mutual interests of Europe and the United States – focused on strengthening global supply chain resilience as a core pillar of global economic stability, security, and long-term growth.

Helena Capital’s investment focus is squarely on security, resilience, and critical national infrastructure, encompassing sectors such as aerospace, advanced manufacturing, and public infrastructure. The firm focuses on the commercialization gap – supporting companies that have moved beyond core invention risk but have not yet reached scaled adoption, helping them bridge into durable revenue, distribution, and long-term market integration. Nelson’s experience directly extends and strengthens this approach.

Nelson’s background prior to a16z further underscores the breadth of her expertise. Earlier in her career, she held influential roles at Gartner, where she advised C-suite executives at Fortune 500 and Global 2000 companies. She guided these organizations through major technology transitions – from the early adoption of cloud computing and mobile to the emergence of artificial intelligence. Beyond her investment and operational work, Nelson maintains an active presence in the academic community as an Executive in Residence and adjunct professor at the NYU Stern School of Business.

Katy Nelson is more than an investor; she is an operator and a proven builder of strategic functions and communities. Her unique combination of deep sector knowledge, operational excellence, and global network will be a meaningful asset as Helena Capital continues to build its reputation as a leading growth investor in critical technology and resilience. The firm looks forward to her leadership and contributions as it enters this next phase of growth.

About Helena Capital

Helena Capital is a transatlantic growth investment firm building the next generation of critical technology companies. With offices in New York and Munich, the firm partners with founders solving foundational challenges in industrial resilience, supply chain security, advanced manufacturing, aerospace, and public infrastructure. Helena Capital invests on the other side of the deep tech ‘valley of death,’ providing growth capital and hands-on operational partnership to revenue-generating companies between Series A and pre-IPO. Learn more at helenacapital.vc.

Dan Chmielewski
Madison Alexander PR (for Helena Capital)
Office: 714-832-8716
Mobile: 949-231-2965
dchm@madisonalexanderpr.com

data.title: Spanish Inflation Rises Further as Iran War Drives Energy Prices Higher — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Don Nico Forbes and Ed Frankl 




  Spain's inflation rate climbed again in April as tensions in the Middle East continued to drive up energy prices. 



  Consumer prices were 3.5% higher than a year earlier, an increase in the annual rate of inflation from 3.4% in March, according to EU-harmonized figures published by Spain's statistics agency INE on Wednesday. That marked the highest inflation level since June 2024, and matched consensus estimates from economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. 



  The inflation rate jumped last month following the outbreak of war in Iran, which sent energy prices skyrocketing across Europe. Prices look set to remain elevated, with uncertainty continuing over the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly a fifth of the world's oil flows. Brent crude oil prices are currently back up above $110 a barrel as talks between the U.S. and Iran remain stalled. 



  The European Union's statistics agency Eurostat is scheduled to publish inflation figures for the overall eurozone on Thursday, shortly before the European Central Bank meets to decide on interest rates. 



  The central bank has said it remains willing to act if necessary to keep inflation in check. But the ECB is widely expected to keep rates on hold as it continues to assess the extent to which higher energy prices are feeding through to core inflation. 



  "There is no easy path back to where we were before this conflict erupted," ECB President Christine Lagarde said in a speech last week. 



  "Households and firms have just lived through a large inflation shock and may be more sensitive to rising costs. The muscle memory is fresh," she said. 



  In separate data published Wednesday, the European Commission's economic sentiment indicator this month sank to its lowest point since November 2020, a time when strict pandemic restrictions were hitting consumers and firms. 



  Lagarde added, however, that higher energy prices and weaker consumer sentiment would weigh on demand, which could limit the extent of price and wage increases. 



  The central bank has previously said a 14% increase in oil prices could drive a rise in the inflation rate of half a percentage point. 



  In late March, the Bank of Spain revised up its inflation expectations for this year and next. It now expects consumer prices to rise by an average of 3.0% in 2026 compared with prior expectations of 2.1%. Inflation is seen at 2.5% in 2027, up from 1.9%. 



  According to the ECB, headline inflation in the eurozone is expected to average 2.6% in 2026, up from 2.1% in 2025 and well above its 2% target. 



  Economists say a prolonged conflict could see consumer prices peak at 4.8% in 2027, while in a less adverse scenario inflation could fall back to the ECB's 2% target next year. 





  Write to Don Nico Forbes at don.forbes@wsj.com and Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 29, 2026 05:49 ET (09:49 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Spice Coated Casing Market to Reach USD 1,254.4 Million by 2036 as Processed Meat Demand and Efficient Seasoning Solutions Drive Growth
data.description:
Spice coated casing market grows as processors adopt solutions that improve seasoning consistency, enhance product appearance, and streamline meat production processes.

NEWARK, DE / ACCESS Newswire / April 29, 2026 / According to the latest analysis by Future Market Insights, the global spice coated casing market is entering a stable growth trajectory as meat processors increasingly adopt coated casing systems to enhance product appearance, streamline seasoning processes, and improve consistency across production batches. Growing demand for value-added meat products, premium sausages, and visually differentiated packaged meats is accelerating the adoption of spice transfer casing technologies.

The market demand stood at USD 640.0 million in 2025 and is projected to reach USD 680.4 million in 2026. With a CAGR of 6.3% from 2026 to 2036, the market is expected to expand to USD 1,254.4 million by 2036, creating an incremental opportunity of USD 574.0 million over the forecast period.

Quick Stats: Spice Coated Casing Market

  • Market Outlook: Expanding application in processed and premium meat products

  • Key Growth Drivers: Demand for consistent surface seasoning, improved product finish, and simplified processing

  • Top Demand Regions: Asia Pacific, North America, Europe

  • Leading Application Segment: Processed meats and sausages

  • Core Product Focus: Natural and collagen-based spice-coated casings

Detailed market forecasts, competitive benchmarking, and pricing trends: https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/sample/rep-gb-7433

Supply Chain Dynamics and Raw Material Considerations

The spice coated casing market relies on dual input streams-casing materials and spice coatings-both of which require consistent quality and reliable sourcing. Variability in casing substrates or spice blends can directly impact coating adhesion, transfer efficiency, and final product uniformity.

Manufacturers are prioritizing tighter control over coating application techniques and casing preparation processes to ensure consistent spice transfer during meat processing. Suppliers that can maintain uniform coating thickness, stable spice blends, and reliable delivery schedules are better positioned to support large-scale industrial demand.

Additionally, integration of coating and casing production processes is becoming increasingly important to reduce variability and enhance overall product performance.

Demand Drivers and Consumer Trends

Market growth is supported by several structural trends:

  • Rising consumption of processed and value-added meat products

  • Increasing focus on product appearance and shelf differentiation

  • Demand for simplified and efficient seasoning processes

  • Growth of premium sausage and specialty meat categories

  • Expansion of branded packaged meat products in retail

Spice coated casings are gaining traction as they enable processors to achieve uniform seasoning distribution without additional handling steps. This improves production efficiency while maintaining a consistent visual and sensory profile.

Product Trends and Segment Insights

The market is segmented by casing material, coating type, end-use product, buyer group, sales channel, and trade format:

  • Natural casings are projected to lead with a 60.0% share in 2026 due to their traditional texture and visual appeal

  • Pepper-coated casings are expected to dominate with a 40.5% share, supported by broad application across meat products

  • Processed meats will account for 37.3% of demand, driven by large-scale production needs

  • B2B processors will hold 72.2% share, reflecting strong industrial demand

  • Direct sales will capture 42.9% share due to the need for technical coordination and supply reliability

  • Retail trade format will contribute 37.5%, supported by shelf appeal and product differentiation

Manufacturers are focusing on solutions that integrate seamlessly into existing production lines while improving consistency and reducing manual intervention.

Processing Constraints and Operational Challenges

Despite steady growth, the market faces several operational challenges:

  • Dependence on dual raw material supply chains (casing and spices)

  • Variability in coating quality and transfer efficiency

  • Need for precise control over coating application

  • Sensitivity to processing conditions and handling

  • Requirement for consistent supply and technical support

Balancing casing integrity with coating performance remains critical, especially for high-volume processors that require repeatable output.

Speak to Analyst: Customize insights for your business strategy: https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/customization-available/rep-gb-7433

Regional Growth Dynamics and Key Markets

Regional demand varies based on meat consumption patterns and processing infrastructure:

  • Asia Pacific: Leading growth driven by expanding food processing sector and rising packaged meat demand

  • North America: Stable demand supported by mature processed meat industry

  • Europe: Moderate growth driven by premium and traditional sausage markets

Country Highlights:

  • India (6.9% CAGR): Fastest-growing market due to expanding food processing industry

  • China (6.7% CAGR): Strong demand driven by large-scale processing capacity

  • Brazil (6.1% CAGR): Growth supported by strong meat production base

  • United States (6.0% CAGR): Mature market driven by product innovation

  • Germany (5.8% CAGR): Demand supported by traditional sausage production

  • Spain (5.6% CAGR): Growth driven by cured and seasoned meat products

  • Japan (4.8% CAGR): Stable demand in a mature market environment

Opportunities in Innovation and Product Development

The evolving market is creating multiple innovation pathways:

  • Improved coating technologies: Enhancing spice adhesion and transfer efficiency

  • Process optimization: Reducing manual seasoning steps in production

  • Premium product positioning: Supporting high-value meat product differentiation

  • Customization: Developing tailored coating blends for specific applications

  • Integrated solutions: Combining casing and coating expertise for better performance

Companies focusing on consistent coating quality, supply reliability, and technical support are likely to gain a competitive edge.

Competitive Landscape

The market is moderately consolidated, with competition centered on casing technology, coating expertise, and supply scale.

Key players include:

  • Viscofan

  • Devro (part of SARIA)

  • Viskase

  • Kalle

  • FABIOS

  • Fibran

  • Shenguan Holdings

Leading companies are strengthening their positions through capacity expansion, product innovation, and integration of coating technologies with casing production.

Unlock 360° insights for strategic decision making and investment planning: https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/checkout/7433

Future Outlook

The spice coated casing market is transitioning into a more structured and scalable segment within the processed meat supply chain. Growth will be driven by the need for consistent product quality, improved production efficiency, and enhanced visual appeal in meat products.

As processors continue to prioritize efficiency and product differentiation, coated casing solutions are expected to play an increasingly important role. Companies that invest in process control, product consistency, and strong customer support will be best positioned to capitalize on emerging opportunities in this evolving market.

Spice Oils and Oleoresins Market: https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/spice-oils-and-oleoresins-market

Vegan Casing Market: https://www.futuremarketinsights.com/reports/vegan-casing-market

Future Market Insights (FMI) delivers actionable, decision-maker-focused research, offering deeper insights beyond traditional market data. Its reports include:

  • Pricing and cost benchmarking

  • Consumer behavior and procurement analysis

  • Supply chain intelligence

FMI combines industry expertise with real-world data inputs to provide practical, validated, and business-ready insights, helping organizations make informed strategic decisions in evolving global markets.

data.title: Thailand's Central Bank Holds as Mideast War Clouds Outlook — Update
data.description:
By Amanda Lee and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa 




  Thailand's central bank held rates steady, returning to the sidelines after two consecutive rate cuts as it gauges the impact of the Middle East war on the country's fragile economy. 



  The Bank of Thailand made its decision as the fallout from the conflict threatens to derail the fledgling recovery seen at the end of last year. 



  As an energy-driven supply shock complicates the outlook for the Southeast Asian economy, the BOT's monetary policy committee voted unanimously to keep its policy rate at 1.00%. 



  The move was expected by all 12 economists polled by The Wall Street Journal, and comes ahead of the Federal Reserve's own decision, which is widely anticipated to be a hold. 



  Like other central banks, the BOT must weigh how to respond to the risk that the war will fuel inflation and slow growth, a task complicated by uncertainty over how long tensions will last. 



  In Wednesday's statement, the central bank struck a cautious tone, acknowledging fresh headwinds. The BOT expects the war to slow growth to 1.5% this year and 2.0% next year as business costs rise and household purchasing power erodes. 



  Higher living costs will weigh on consumption and travel constraints will hinder tourism--a key growth pillar. 



  The central bank projects inflation in 2026 to average 2.9%, the highest since 2022, when the war in Ukraine triggered a global supply shock and the BOT delivered a series of rate hikes. 



  This time, however, the central bank doesn't expect price increases to be broad-based or persistent as weak demand caps inflationary pressures. 



  Inflation expectations remain anchored for now, policymakers said, while keeping a close watch on energy prices and supply disruptions amid the Middle East conflict. 



  Asian economies like Thailand rely heavily on the Middle East for energy and key commodities such as fertilizers, leaving them vulnerable to oil and food supply shocks. Still, growth could exceed expectations on potential additional government stimulus, the central bank said. Exports are also projected to grow, supported by global technology demand. 



  What the central bank does next will depend largely on how the Middle East situation develops, though many economists see limited room to maneuver. 



  The central banks of Singapore and the Philippines have tightened policy settings to cushion against inflation risks. Thailand's central bank, however, doesn't seem to have the same flexibility, HSBC economist Aris Dacanay said. 



  "The BOT will likely look for concrete evidence first of a wage-price spiral before taking any action and kickstarting its tightening cycle," Dacanay said. 



  Barclays expects Thailand to stay on hold throughout the year. The central bank may be willing to look through a short-term breach of inflation target, with price pressures likely coming from the supply-side, it said. 





  Write to Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 29, 2026 05:31 ET (09:31 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Finland's Kone to Buy German Rival TK Elevator For Around $24 Billion — 2nd Update
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping 




  Finland's Kone agreed to buy German rival TK Elevator for around $23.66 billion from a consortium led by private-equity firms Advent and Cinven in a deal that would create the world's biggest elevator maker by sales. 



  Under the deal, Kone will pay the consortium around 5 billion euros ($5.86 billion) in cash and up to 270 million newly issued Kone shares, which currently have a total value of around 15.2 billion euros. 



  Including debt, the deal values TK Elevator at around 29.4 billion euros. 



  The combined group is set to be headquartered in Finland and generate annual sales of around 20.5 billion euros, employing more than 100,000 people, Kone said. 



  In 2025, elevator giant Otis Worldwide posted sales of $14.4 billion, while Schindler Holding's sales came in at $13.9 billion. 



  Annual synergies from the deal are seen at 700 million euros. 



  "This combination would meaningfully enhance our ability to meet customers' growing demand for reliable and sustainable solutions and services in a rapidly evolving environment," said Kone Chief Executive Philippe Delorme. 



  "It would also accelerate our strategic shift to service and modernization, reinforcing our resilience." 



  Kone expects the transaction to close by the second quarter of 2027 at the earliest, but analysts have cautioned that the deal consolidates a market with four big players into three, so it would likely face regulatory hurdles. 



  Aapeli Pursimo, an analyst at Inderes, said the deal represents the largest corporate acquisition in Finnish history and would create a combined group that would clearly be the largest company in the industry, but approval by competition authorities and the duration of the process are big unknowns. 



  "In particular in the European market, overlaps may lead to demands for the sale of businesses," he said. 



  Kone CEO Delorme sought to ease investor concerns on an investor call, saying the company had done its homework before launching the deal and it was confident of receiving all regulatory approvals while preserving the strategic rationale of the deal. 



  "We are prepared to work constructively with regulators to ensure full compliance," he said. 



  Wednesday's proposal comes several years after Kone first tried to acquire the TK Elevator business. It had been in talks with Thyssenkrupp to buy the business in 2020 as part of a joint bid with CVC Capital Partners, before deciding against the move. The business was instead sold to a consortium led by Advent and Cinven for over 17 billion euros. 



  TK Elevator, whose elevators can be found in New York's One World Trade Center, the tallest building in the U.S., became an independent company following its separation from Thyssenkrupp in 2020. Thyssenkrupp retains a stake of just over 16% in TKE as of Sept. 30, according to its latest annual report. 



  Kone CEO Delorme, Chief Financial Officer Ilkka Hara and Chairman Antti Herlin would take on their respective positions in the new combined group. 





  Write to Dominic Chopping at dominic.chopping@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 29, 2026 05:42 ET (09:42 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: ICBC Quarterly Net Profit Rises — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka 




  Industrial & Commercial Bank of China reported a rise in first-quarter net profit, supported by lower interest expenses, even as it booked higher credit costs. 



  The Chinese bank said Wednesday that net profit rose 3.3% from a year earlier to 86.94 billion yuan, equivalent to $12.72 billion, for the three months ended March. 



  The lender said net interest income--the difference between interest earned on loans and that paid on deposits--increased 7.5% to 168.53 billion yuan, partly due to lower interest expenses. 



  Net fee and commission income was also higher, climbing 5.2% to 40.92 billion yuan. 



  The bank booked credit impairment losses of 69.29 billion yuan, wider than the 56.97 billion yuan in the year-earlier period. 



  The lender's nonperforming-loan ratio was 1.31% at the end of March, unchanged from the end of 2025. 





  Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 29, 2026 05:26 ET (09:26 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: DCC Shares Jump on KKR Consortium Takeover Approach
data.description:
By Ian Walker 




  Marketing and support-services provider DCC PLC's shares rose after it said it received an indicative cash takeover offer from Energy Capital Partners and Kohlberg Kravis Roberts & Co. that it's reviewing. 



  The FTSE 100-listed company didn't provide any details of the indicated offer price and advised shareholders Wednesday not to take any action at this time. 



  Shares were up 715 pence, or 13%, at 60.95 pence in late morning European trading, implying a market capitalization of 5.21 billion pounds ($7.04 billion). Shares are up 32% over the year to date. 



  The consortium has until June 10 to either make a formal offer to buy DCC or walk away under Irish Takeover rules. 





  Write to Ian Walker at ian.walker@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 29, 2026 05:29 ET (09:29 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: European Midday Briefing: Oil Gains As Investors Await U.S. Earnings, Fed Decision
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

Despite some solid gains for individual companies, shares in Europe were down-with luxuries, retail, industrials and utilities the sharpest falling sectors-and oil was higher.

Investors were weighing the prospect of an extended U.S.-Iran conflict against the UAE's exit from OPEC.

The U.A.E. said it would also exit OPEC+--a group that includes Russia-and gradually increase production afterward. However, that might not come soon enough to avoid a worsening oil crunch.

"While...crude oil futures have dropped off their March highs, the physical supply squeeze for oil may be somewhat underappreciated by investors," LPL Financial said.

"Early-year oversupply has helped absorb the immediate shock better than feared, while markets still face normalization that could take months."

The situation was also the key variable in rates markets, Strategy Asset Managers said, and futures pointing to oil above $85 were keeping inflation expectations elevated and driving daily volatility in Treasury yields.

The bond market would stay sensitive to inflation risk as long as the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz remained, it added.

"That's why we're still hearing discussion around potential rate hikes later this year."

The Federal Reserve is expected to hold rates steady in its decision at 1800 GMT.

U.S. Markets:

Stock futures were muted ahead of a massive day for the market, with a slew of Big Tech earnings likely to decide if the April rally has more room to run.

Alphabet, Amazon, Meta Platforms, and Microsoft are set to report after the closing bell and all four are plowing money into AI, so their capital spending guidance could help ease the recent jitters.

Forex:

The euro was lower against the dollar and ING said the Fed's decision and U.S.-Iran tensions could extend the euro's current losses.

Traders remain inclined to buy the euro when it falls below $1.17-which has emerged as a key benchmark level for sentiment on the Middle East among foreign exchange investors-it said.

The euro could see a more decisive break below that level if the Fed leaned in favor of tight monetary policy, risk sentiment was poor and there was a lack of progress toward reopening the Strait of Hormuz, it added.

The dollar rose as oil prices remained elevated.

Meanwhile, the Fed is expected to leave interest rates unchanged, a decision Commerzbank said wasn't likely to have much impact on the dollar.

Bonds:

Eurozone government bonds yields edged higher and economic data on Wednesday and Thursday, ahead of the European Central Bank's meeting, had the potential to move yields.

Global bond markets were reacting to risks to the global economy from the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the uncertain timing on its reopening, Ofi Invest AM said.

"Bond markets have accordingly had to price in the inflationary risks that are being driven by higher oil prices."

This risk, which would send interest rates up, comes with risks of a recession, which would drive rates downward.

The probability of a stalemate under a stagflation scenario rose during the month, it said, adding that this would pose a quandary for central banks, as they may have to raise key rates and then lower them later.

According to Strategy Asset Managers, oil likely needs to settle back into the $70 range for yields to normalize.

"Once energy stabilizes, inflation expectations should follow, allowing Treasuries to revert toward more fundamental levels."

Nuveen reckoned that the Fed's meeting was unlikely to be the catalyst to break the 10-year Treasury yield from its recent tight trading range--as not even oil prices were now moving the needle.

" Next week's payrolls could be the pivot ."

Energy:

Oil prices remained elevated after Trump instructed aides to prepare for an extended blockade of Iran.

"Stalled peace talks have raised the prospect of an indefinite disruption to oil supplies from the Persian Gulf," ANZ said.

"The rebalancing of the market once the Strait of Hormuz is reopened will take years."

Traders are now watching for updates on peace negotiations and this week's U.S. crude inventory report for signals on how quickly stockpiles are declining amid strong export demand.

Phillip Nova said prices could extend gains if disruptions to maritime traffic through the Strait of Hormuz continued, lifting expectations for tighter supply.

Metals:

Gold futures were edging lower.

Prices have declined nearly 11% since the Iran war began, but the World Gold Council said persistent geopolitical uncertainty was still expected to drive investment demand and central-bank buying this year .

Goldman Sachs maintained a bullish outlook for gold, forecasting prices to reach $5,400 an ounce by the end of the year.

Phillip Nova expects the next downside support level for gold to be around $4,500 a troy ounce, followed by $4,350.

EMEA HEADLINES

Spanish Inflation Rises Further as Iran War Drives Energy Prices Higher

Spain's inflation rate climbed again in April as tensions in the Middle East continued to drive up energy prices.

Consumer prices were 3.5% higher than a year earlier, an increase in the annual rate of inflation from 3.4% in March, according to EU-harmonized figures published by Spain's statistics agency INE on Wednesday. That marked the highest inflation level since June 2024, and matched consensus estimates from economists polled by The Wall Street Journal.

AstraZeneca Gets Revenue Boost From Cancer Drugs

AstraZeneca reported rises in revenue and core earnings for the first quarter, saying growing sales of its cancer and rare-disease drugs lifted its results.

The U.K. drugmaker said Wednesday that it generated $15.29 billion in revenue compared with $13.59 billion in the year-earlier period, with an 8% rise when excluding currency changes. Analysts had forecast revenue at $14.94 billion, according to consensus estimates compiled by Visible Alpha.

Kone to Buy TK Elevator for Around $24 Billion

Finland's Kone agreed to buy German rival TK Elevator for around $23.66 billion from a consortium led by private-equity firms Advent and Cinven in a deal that would create the world's biggest elevator maker by sales.

Under the deal, Kone will pay the consortium around 5 billion euros in cash and up to 270 million in newly issued Kone shares, which currently have a total value of around EUR15.2 billion.

GSK Earnings Boosted by Higher Specialty Medicine Sales

GSK reported higher earnings on increased specialty medicine sales and said it expects to pay a total dividend this year of 70 pence a share.

The FTSE 100-listed pharmaceutical giant said Wednesday that core operating profit-a company preferred metric-for the first quarter was 2.65 billion pounds ($3.58 billion) compared with 2.53 billion pounds for the same period a year earlier, and a consensus of 2.46 billion pounds, taken from the company's website.

TotalEnergies Increases Shareholder Returns as Iran Conflict Boosts Earnings

TotalEnergies said it would return more cash to shareholders as the conflict in the Middle East provides an earnings windfall.

The French energy major said Wednesday that it would resume share repurchases of up to $1.5 billion over the second quarter ending June as war-induced price rises deliver earnings and cash-flow growth. It also raised its interim dividend by nearly 6% to 0.90 euros ($1.05) a share.

UBS Net Profit Climbs on Boost From Investment-Banking, Wealth Units

UBS Group reported a sharply higher net profit for the first quarter, benefiting from strong performances across its investment-banking and wealth-management arms.

The Swiss bank said Wednesday that it remains on track to deliver on its objectives for 2026, as the group approaches the end of its integration of Credit Suisse while it tries to soften the blow from Switzerland's plans to revamp its banking rules.

Spain's Santander Profit Climbs Amid Global Portfolio Reshuffle

Spain's Banco Santander reported a surge in profit as it added more customers and logged a large gain from a recent disposal.

Continental Europe's largest lender by market capitalization posted a 60% increase in first-quarter net profit to 5.455 billion euros ($6.39 billion). The bottom line was helped by a 1.9 billion-euro capital gain from the recently completed sale of most of its Polish subsidiary.

Lloyds Banking Group Posts Rise in Profit on Loan Growth

Lloyds Banking Group said its profit rose on continued loan growth and support from its structural hedge.

The U.K.'s largest mortgage provider on Wednesday posted 2.025 billion pounds ($2.74 billion) in pretax profit for the first quarter, beating a 1.84 billion-pound estimate taken from a company-compiled consensus.

Danish Brewer Carlsberg Books Boost From Soft Drinks, Premium Beers

Carlsberg said higher sales of pricier beer and soft drinks boosted volumes and revenue at the start of the year, adding to tentative signs of recovery for the world's brewers as drinkers shrug off conflict in the Middle East.

The Danish brewer behind Tuborg, 1664 and its namesake label said it had seen no major changes in consumer behavior over the year's first three months, despite a "volatile and uncertain" geopolitical environment.

Deutsche Bank Posts Rise in Profit on Stable Performance

Deutsche Bank reported a rise in its quarterly profit on the steady performance of its investment bank and growth in other divisions.

Germany's largest lender by total assets on Wednesday posted an after-tax profit of 2.17 billion euros ($2.55 billion) for the first quarter, 8% higher than a year prior and ahead of consensus expectations of 2.005 billion euros.

data.title: European Midday Briefing: Oil Gains As Investors Await U.S. Earnings, Fed Decision -2-
data.description:
For the past several years, millions of disenchanted young people in China have embraced a mindset dubbed "lying flat." In an economy with limited well-paying jobs, many have rejected societal pressures to overwork or have stopped working completely.

On Tuesday, the country's intelligence agency blamed anti-China influences from abroad for stoking youth disillusionment.
